California
Fair P01itical
Practices Commission
December 24 | l99O
Mr. R. Charl-es Harker, Esq.
American Physical Therapy Association
1L 11 North Fairfax street
Alexandria, Virginia 
223L4
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. A-90-691
Dear Mr. Ha\.rker:
You have requested advice concerning _the campaign provisions
of the Po1itical Reform Act (the tta61r;.1/
FAqTS
The American Physical Therapy Association (ApTA) is a
national membership organization with chapters in every state.
A11 California menbers of AEITA are also members of the California
Chapter-APTA. CAL-PT-PAC is a sponsored cornmittee of the
Ca1 ifornia Chapter-APTA.
AETA membership dues fron california rnembers incl,ude a pro-
rata share of dues to the California Chapter-ApTA. APTA is
considering adding a line itern to the dues invoice to enable
nembers to earmark an additional amount of funds to CAL-PT-PAC.
.In a telephone conversation with me on November 20, 1990, you
confirrned that nembership payrnents are rnailed to a bank,s 1ock box
and deposited to AHtArs account the day the bank receives the
palment or the next banking day. Later, the bank provides APTA
with an accounting surnmary of the rnenbership dues received.
Followj.ng such notification the appropriate pro-rata share of
California dues are forwarded to the California Chapter-ApTA.
APTA would also forward funds earmarked for CAL-PT-PAC.
l/ 
covernment code sections 81ooo-91015. Al-r statutory
references are to the Governnent Code unLess otherwise indicated.
Comrnission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. A11 references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

Mr. R. C. Harker′
 Esq.
December 24′  ■990
Page 2
OUESTIONS
■.   Is there an advantage for APTA to become a sponsor of
CAL― PT― PAC?
2.   Under the circumstances described above′
 wou■d APTA be
cons■dered an 
■nte..llediary or an agent and what is the difference
between these designations?
3.   What would be the date of receipt for contributions made
to CAL― PT― PAC?
4.   What would be the deadline for APTA to report
contributions to cAL―
PT― PAC?
5. 
will APTA or CAL-PT-PAC be required by the Act to obtain
employer information for contributors to CAL-PT-PAC?
6. 
If APTA does not learn of the receipt of a contribution
to CAL-PT-PAC on the closing date of the reporting period and
provides the information at a later date, would any liability
arise from the filing by CAL-PT-PAC of an amended carnpaign
statement?
7. 
Is it possible for the Commission
examples of how other national associations
collection of membership and politica). dues
affiliates?
to provide APTA
adninister the
for their state
CONCII'SIONS
1 and 2. 
Because APTA will receive earnarked contributions
for CAL-PT-PAC, APTA will becorne both a sponsor and an agent of
CAL-PT-PAC. In addition, APTA wilL act as an intermediary on
behalf of CAL-PT-PAC contributors. 
Please refer to the analysis
belo!, for a discussion of sponsor, internediary, and agent
responsibilities .
3. 
The date of receipt of contributions to CAL-PT-PAC is
the date the rnernbership dues are deposited into ApTA,s bank
account .
4. 
APTA must report contributions to CAL-PT-PAC no later
than the closing date of the canpaign statement on which the
contributions nust be disclosed by CAL-PT-PAC.


Mr. R. C. Harker, Esq.
December 24, L99O
Page 3
5. 
APTA must obtain the name, address,
employer of CAL-PT-PAC contributors.
6. 
when an error or omission occurs on
canpaign statement, CAL-PT-PAC j-s required to
statenent. There are no penalties for filing
statement.
7.   For yOur reference, I have enclosed two advice 
■etters
from assOciations which have made inqu■
ries similar to thOse

iCe Letter′  No. A-89-495 and
ANALYSIS
An organization which receives political contributions
totaling s■′000 or more ■n a calendar year for the purpOse of
making contr■butions or expenditures to suppOrt or oppose
California state or 
■ocal candidates or measures is defined in the
Act as a recipient :'coIIlmittee.1:  (sectiOn 320■
3(a)。) A recipient
COIIlmittee is requ■red to file a statement of organizatiOn (Form
4■0)and′  among other things, identify a■
■ sponsors of the
committee。  (sectiOns 84■
0■′ 84■02.)
Section 82048.7 defines rrsponsored committeer as follows:
(q) rrsponsored cornmj-tteett means a committee, other than
a candidate controLled committee, which has one or more
sponsors. Any person, except a candidate or other
individual, nay sponsor a comnittee.
(!) 
A person sponsors a conmittee if any of the
following apply:
(1) The conrnittee receives a1l or nearly aI1 of its
contributions from the person or its nenbers, officers,
employees, or shareholders.
(2) The person collects contributiOns for the cOmmittee
by use of pavroll deductiOns or dues from 
■ts members,
officers. 
。r emplovees.
(3) The person alone or in conbination with other
organizations, provides al1 or nearly all of the
admi-nistrative services for the comn-itte".
(4) The person, alone or in combination with other
organizations, sets the policies for sol-iciting coniiiU"tion=
or making expenditures of committee funds.
occupation and
a previously filed
file an amended
an amended
(Emphasis added . )


Mr. R. C. Harker, Esq.
December 24, L99O
Page 4
Pursuant to Section a2o49.7, because APIA will collect
contributions from its rnernbers on behalf of CAL-PT-PAC, APTA r./ill
become a sponsor. Accordingly, within LO days of APTA becorning a
sponsor, CAL-PT-PAC must amend its statement of organization ( Forrn
410) . 
(Section 841-o3. )
Since a portion of the members, dues received by APTA will 
be
rrearmarkedrr for CAL-PT-PAC, APTA will act as an intermediary for
the nembers' contributions. 
In addition, because APTA lrill 
be
soliciting 
and receiving contributions on behatf of cAL-pT-pAc,
APIA will be acting as an agent for the committee. (Section
a43O2, Zweiq Advice Letter, No. A-83-228; and crooks Advice
Letter, No. A-89-464, copi-es enclosed.) Therefore, APIA wi1I have
responsibil ities under the Act as both an intermediary and an
agent, as discussed below.
The l-99O Infornation }lanual C--Canpaicrn provisions of the
Political 
Reform Act for ceneral Eurpose Recipient Comnittees
(copy enclosed) states:
A contribution is |treceivedtr the day the candidate
or connittee or his or her aqent actually receives
the contribution, not when the contribution is deposited
into the comittee,s bank account.
Infornation Manual C, p. 48
(Enphasis added. )
Therefore, CAL-PT-PAC rrreceivesr the contributions on the
date APTA receives the nenbers, dues. Under the facts you have
provided, the date of receipt would be the date a contr-ibution is
credited to APTATS bank account.
.With regard to recordkeeping responsibil ities, section 84306
provides:
...A11 contributions received by a person acting as an
agent of a committee shall be reported promptl-y to the
conmittee,s treasurer or any of his or her aesignated
agents. rrPromptlytr as used in this section rneans not
later than the closing date of any campaign statenent
the comrnittee or candidate for r+hom thL contribution is
intended is reguired to fi1e.


Mr. R. C. Harker, Esq.
December 24, L99O
Page 5
Accordingly, all contributions received by AHrA must be
reported to CAL-PT-PAC no later than the closing date of any
campaign statement the cornmj-ttee is reguired to file. 
If APTA
becomes aware of the receipt of a contribution at a later date,
APTA must pronptly notify CAL-PT-PAC. FoIlolring such
notification, 
CAL-PT-PAC nust file an amended siatement disclosing
the contribution.
Any report or statement filed pursuant to this title
may be amended by the filer at any tine. 
Arnending an
incorrect or incomplete report or statement may bt
considered as evidence of good faith.
Section 81004.5.
The Act imposes no penalties for the filing of an amendrnent.
Cornmittee treasurers are required to retain the name,
address, occupation and ernployer of all persons fron $/hom a
contribution of $25 or more ii received.2/ (Regulation 18401_.)
In addition, the conrnittee must disclose on the campaign statenent
the name, address, occupation and employer of each contributor of
$100 or more. Since APTA is both an interrnediary and an agent for
contributions to CAL-PI-PAC, APTA must provide to Caf,-pt-peC aft
of the information the conrnittee is required to maintain and
discLose. (sections 84302 and A42LL-) However, if APTA and cAL-
|T:PAC have an agreement that CAL-FT-pAC will obtain employer
inforrnation for contributors, and cAL-pT-pAc does in talt -oUtain
and properly disclose the required information, APTA s/ould not
also be required to obtain such information.
_In addition, when a sponsor, acting as an intermediary,
provides its sponsored committee vith total contributions of $foo
or nore, the sponsor must be disclosed on the comnittee,s campaign
statement. (Regulation 18419 (c) (3) . )
At your request, I have enclosed advice letters which address
questions sirnilar to those discussed in this letter.
At its January 9, L991 meeting, the Cornmission will
reconsider whether cornnittees must retain occupation and
information for contributors of less g1oo. you may wish
contact our office regarding this requirenent after that
empl oye r
to
date.

December 24, L99O

Scott Hallabrin
